Learning Python from a JavaScript Angle (31): So What Do You Want to Learn Next?
Python
JavaScript
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle

I finally crossed the finish line of the Ironman contest. So for day 31, the last one, I'm just going to sit down and chat with you about how the whole thing went — what I learned about Python coming from JavaScript, and why I think the real takeaway is to keep learning no matter what tool you pick up.
/en/python/2021/09/30/python-29-beautifulsoup/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (29) - BeautifulSoup
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle

We're heading into the home stretch of the Ironman contest, so this post covers a really common package: BeautifulSoup. It's one of the go-to tools for web scraping in Python, used to parse the HTML you download so you can pull out exactly the bits you want. Let's learn how to use it.
/en/python/2021/09/27/learning-python-from-javascript-26-shebang/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (26) - What That #! Line at the Top Actually Means

We've covered a lot of heavy stuff already, so let's take it easy this time and talk about something simple. You've probably seen that weird #!/usr/bin/env python line at the top of Python files. What is it? It's called a shebang, and here's what it does and why you'd want it.
/en/python/2021/09/26/python-25-exception-handling/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (25) - Exception Handling

Next up is something really, really important: handling errors, which we usually call exception handling. We'll look at try/except in Python, how to target specific error types, what finally is for, and Python's raise (the equivalent of JavaScript's throw), all compared side by side with JavaScript.
/en/python/2021/09/24/python-from-js-23-class/
Learning Python From a JS Angle (23): Classes — and Why JavaScript Isn't Quite OOP

A quick, low-key chat about Python's class syntax. Along the way we untangle a question that bugged me for ages: is JavaScript actually object-oriented? Spoiler — kind of, but not the way you think. Then we build a Python class step by step, meet __init__ and self, and see how close it all feels to JS.
/en/python/2021/09/19/js-to-python-18-file-handling/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (18): File Handling

This time we're going to talk about doing some file handling in Python — things like creating a file and writing data into a specific file. We'll cover open(), the different modes (r, w, a), reading and writing, and at the end the "plus" modes like r+ that let you read and write at the same time.
/en/python/2021/09/13/learn-python-from-javascript-12-operators/
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle (12) - Operators

Operators are something you use constantly, so there's no skipping them in the basics. We'll look at the arithmetic operators, the surprising Python 2 vs Python 3 division difference, the floor division operator, merging lists with +, and the operator precedence gotcha that shows up in JavaScript interviews.
/en/python/2021/09/12/learning-python-from-javascript-11-list-20-operations/
Learning Python From a JS Dev's View (11): 20 Ways to Work With Lists
Learning Python From a JavaScript Perspective

Now that we've covered dictionaries and lists, I wanted to take a lighter, no-pressure chapter to walk through Python's list operations. JavaScript has about twenty array methods, and as it turns out, Python has roughly the same number. Here they are, split into the ones that mutate the original list and the ones that don't, each with a quick example.
/en/python/2021/09/11/js-to-python-10-container-types-part-2/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (10): Container Types, Part 2

Last time we played around with some fun ways to pull values out of a dict. This time I want to keep going with dictionaries — keys, values, items — and then take a detour into tuples, since they show up the moment you call items(). Plus a quick look at Python's in operator and how it compares to JS.
/en/python/2021/09/09/js-to-python-08-bmi-calculator-1/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (8) - BMI Calculator (1)

Time to harvest what we've covered so far with a little exercise. I like to drop a small practice project every few chapters so this series isn't just reading about Python — building something tiny makes it stick. This time it's the classic BMI calculator, written first in JavaScript and then in Python, plus a look at Python's string formatting.
